text: The surgeon begins dissection laterally, creating space and working anteriorly to take down the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon uses cautery on the medial umbilical ligament, applying energy to this anatomical structure.
The surgeon exposes the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, appearing thin on one side medially, then crosses back over to connect the tissue planes.
The surgeon works laterally, maintaining a thin dissection on the peritoneal flap, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon pushes the fat tissue laterally to cover the exposed nerves, ensuring they are protected during the procedure.
The surgeon approaches the deep internal ring, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels located superiorly and to the right, indicating the presence of an indirect hernia.
The surgeon exposes the iliopubic tract, revealing its location and orientation within the surgical field.
The iliopubic tract is visible, originating from the anterior superior iliac spine.
The iliopubic tract is visible running medially and terminating at the pubic tubercle.
The surgeon carefully dissects around a large cord lipoma, revealing the underlying iliopubic tract where nerves are expected to run.
The surgeon attempts to first isolate the sac and then the cord lipoma, but both structures are being brought down simultaneously during the dissection.
The surgeon applies touch cautery to further dissect and pull down the cord lipoma, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon examines the iliopubic tract, ensuring that the nerves are not being inadvertently pulled into the specimen during the dissection.
The surgeon observes the tissue extending far but notes that it separates cleanly from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies upward and lateral traction, pulling the tissue towards the trocar during the procedure.
The surgeon exposes the spermatic vessels, which are visible beneath the surrounding tissue.
The vas deferens comes into view, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the hernia sac and uses a milking technique to carefully separate it from the underlying structures.
The surgeon truncates the cord lipoma, allowing some of the fat to lay laterally to avoid drifting into the nearby nerves.
The surgeon identifies a cord lipoma and prepares to remove it, with the lipoma visibly situated near the spermatic cord.
The surgeon continues to dissect the hernia sac, creating additional space by carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon positions the instruments near the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, ensuring clear visibility for precise needle placement.
The surgeon carefully manipulates tissue around the iliac vein, ensuring precise handling in the surgical field.
The surgeon maintains a visible plane during dissection, ensuring clear separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon places a mesh over the indirect inguinal hernia and begins suturing on the side of the hernia. The mesh is fixated with high lateral sutures to ensure it remains deep within the pocket.
The surgeon has placed one Cooper's ligament fixation and one high lateral fixation, ensuring the mesh remains securely positioned.
The surgeon performs a standard flap closure over the surgical site.
